In addition to textual aspects, a proofreader holds a crucial responsibility in inspecting the "physical mechanics" of a book that has reached the layout stage. Suzanne Gilad emphasizes that a proofreader must be vigilant against typographical errors known as "bad breaks." This includes widows (the last line of a paragraph appearing alone at the top of a new page) and orphans (a single word or the first line of a paragraph left stranded at the bottom of a page). Such visual disruptions, while grammatically correct, can compromise the aesthetics and professionalism of a publication.
A proofreader must also watch for "rivers of white," which are gaps between words that accidentally align vertically or diagonally across several consecutive lines, potentially distracting the reader. They verify that running heads (chapter titles at the top of the page) accurately reflect the content of that chapter and that page numbers (folios) are strictly sequential without any interruptions.
Regarding workflow, proofreaders often employ "double-reading" techniques. In traditional publishing, this involves a copyholder reading the original manuscript aloud—explicitly mentioning punctuation—while the proofreader checks the printed proofs. In the digital era, they are also responsible for ensuring that all hyperlinks (URLs) are active and direct to the correct sites, as well as verifying that the book’s index accurately refers to the correct page numbers following layout adjustments. This role serves as the final gatekeeper for the author’s and publisher’s reputation; once a manuscript is printed, any remaining errors become permanent.

Saat saya SMA saya menyukai salah satu teman sekelas saya. kami sempat dekat beberapa tahun, sampai suatu hari dia mempunyai seorang pacar dan dia meninggalkan ku, tetapi ketika dia putus dengan pacarnya dia tetap balik kepada saya untuk menceritakan semuanya. Setelah lulus SMA, dia melanjutkan pendidikannya di luar negeri sehingga kami tidak pernah lagi berkomunikasi sampai saat ini, dan kemarin saya mendapatkan kabar dari sahabat saya jika dia sudah memiliki istri dan mereka sudah menikah selama 3 tahun. lagu ini selalu mengigatkan saya kpd pria itu, makasi niki udh membuat lagu ini.... semoga segera move on dan menyusulnya ke jenjang pernikahan. In another life semoga kami berdualah yg duduk di pelaminan itu.

i just realised by watching this video that it's not about an Ex, but it's about your past self. And I'll tell you why

"I don't like anyone except sometimes you"
"You look ugly when you cry / But I'm the one you think to call"
It sounds like a toxic but intimate connection — and no one knows you like your past self. The you could be a younger version of the speaker — broken, coping, codependent.

"Guess I won't ever mind / Crisping up on your backburner"
If the past self represents old trauma, patterns, or mindsets, being “on the backburner” could mean always simmering in the background, never fully healed. The speaker keeps circling back to that version of themselves.

"Maybe I blame my mother bleedin' into my stride
Maybe it was my father and his wandering eyes"
This is the speaker exploring how they became who they are, as if they’re tracing the emotional family tree that grew their past self. It’s self-reflection disguised as blaming another.

"Maybe life's less romantic when I don't wanna die"
That’s so specific. It sounds like a version of themselves that was dramatic, hurt, depressed — but maybe even addicted to the chaos. And now that they've grown a bit, life feels dull without the pain, like something’s missing.
Could be mourning the intensity of that old self, even if it was unhealthy.

So Is It About an Ex or About “Me”?
Maybe both.
Maybe the ex is the past self.
Maybe the “you” is the part of us that we keep trying to outgrow, but secretly miss.
